perturbation.original_text: Each party shall at all times comply with the provisions of all applicable laws and the rules and regulations thereunder, and refrain from engaging in any illegal, unfair or deceptive trade practices or unethical business practices whatsoever with respect to the promotion and sale or service of Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall endeavor to comply with the provisions of all applicable laws and the rules and regulations thereunder, and shall make reasonable efforts to refrain from engaging in any illegal, unfair or deceptive trade practices or unethical business practices whatsoever with respect to the promotion and sale or service of Products.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces the mandatory 'shall comply' with the weaker 'shall endeavor to comply' and 'make reasonable efforts to refrain,' creating ambiguity as to the level of commitment to legal compliance. This weakens the obligation and makes enforcement more difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17200 of the California Business and Professions Code prohibits unfair competition, including unlawful business practices. The original text requires compliance, aligning with the strict requirements of this law. The modified text uses ambiguous language ('endeavor to comply,' 'reasonable efforts'), potentially allowing a party to argue that they made an effort to comply, even if they violated the law, thus contradicting the strict liability nature of Section 17200.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity into the compliance obligation, weakening the original, more stringent requirement. The original text states "Each party shall at all times comply", which is a clear and direct mandate. The modified text uses "endeavor to comply" and "make reasonable efforts to refrain", which introduces a subjective element and allows for interpretation. This directly contradicts the strict nature of California Business and Professions Code Section 17200, which prohibits unfair competition, including unlawful business practices as stated in snippet 1: "unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice". By weakening the compliance language, the modified text creates a scenario where a party could argue they 'endeavored' or made 'reasonable efforts' even if their actions constituted an unlawful business practice, undermining the legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Zanotti shall maintain records of all Products sold by Zanotti and shall immediately alert Aura in the event that Zanotti becomes aware of any defect in or hazard posed by any Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Zanotti shall generally maintain records of Products, and should they become aware of a potential defect or hazard posed by any Product, Zanotti should notify Aura.
perturbation.explanation: The change replaces 'shall maintain records of all Products sold' with 'shall generally maintain records of Products,' weakening the obligation to maintain comprehensive records. Additionally, 'shall immediately alert Aura' is replaced by 'Zanotti should notify Aura,' reducing the urgency and mandatory nature of the notification. The term potential is ambiguous, reducing the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Health and Safety Code Section 109875
perturbation.law_explanation: This section mandates reporting requirements when businesses become aware of health hazards related to their products. The original text's obligation to 'immediately alert' is more in line with the intention of ensuring swift action to protect public health. The modified 'Zanotti should notify' is more lax and ambiguous, potentially delaying necessary reports and thus contradicting the purpose of the law. Also, replacing 'defect or hazard' with 'potential defect or hazard' adds another layer of interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, HSC 109875 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Health and Safety Code - HSC DIVISION 104. ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH [106500 - 119406] ( Division 104 added by Stats. 1995, Ch. 415, Sec. 6. ) PART 5. SHERMAN FOOD, DRUG, AND COSMETIC LAWS [109875 - 111929.4] ( Part 5 added by Stats. 1995, Ch. 415, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions and Definitions [109875 - 110040] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1995, Ch. 415, Sec. 6. ) 109875. This part shall be known as the Sherman Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Law. (Added by Stats. 1995, Ch. 415, Sec. 6. Effective January 1, 1996.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain records and report defects, creating an ambiguous legal obligation. The original text requires Zanotti to 'immediately alert' Aura of any defect or hazard, which aligns with ensuring swift action. The modified text replaces this with 'Zanotti should notify,' introducing ambiguity and potentially delaying necessary reports. The scraped law snippet 1, refers to the Sherman Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Law, implying a need for businesses to meet obligations to ensure product safety and consumer protection. By weakening the language from 'shall immediately alert' to 'should notify' and adding the word 'potential', the change introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation less enforceable, creating a legal contradiction as defined.

perturbation.original_text: In the event any party desires to issue any press release or public announcement concerning any provisions of this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby, such party shall so advise the other party hereto, and the parties shall thereafter use commercially reasonable efforts to cause a mutually agreeable release or announcement to be issued.
perturbation.changed_text: If a party considers issuing a press release or public announcement concerning this Agreement, they may attempt to advise the other party, and the parties could then try to use reasonable efforts to issue a mutually agreeable release or announcement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause stipulates a clear process: a party *shall* advise the other before issuing a press release and *shall* use commercially reasonable efforts to achieve a mutually agreeable release. The modified version weakens this by replacing 'shall' with phrases like 'If a party considers,' 'they may attempt to advise,' and 'parties could then try to use reasonable efforts'. This makes the entire process discretionary and less binding.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation FD aims to prevent selective disclosure of material nonpublic information. While this contract clause isn't directly about selective disclosure, the weakened requirement to collaborate on press releases introduces a loophole. A party could release information without proper coordination, potentially leading to selective disclosure and violating the spirit of Regulation FD. The original text's stronger collaboration requirement provided better safeguards against this.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: st or confidence for purposes of the "misappropriation" theory of insider trading under Section 10(b) of the Act and Rule 10b-5. The law of insider trading is otherwise defined by judicial opinions construing Rule 10b-5, and Rule 10b5-2 does not modify the scope of insider trading law in any other respect. (a) Scope of Rule . This section shall apply to any violation of Section 10(b) of the Act (15 U.S.C. 78j(b)) and § 240.10b-5 thereunder that is based on the purchase or sale of securities on the basis of, or the communication of, material nonpublic information misappropriated in breach of a duty of trust or confidence. (b) Enumerated "duties of trust or confidence." For purposes of this section, a "duty of trust or confidence" exists in the following circumstances, among others: (1) Whenever a person agrees to maintain information in confidence; (2) Whenever the person communicating the material nonpublic information and the person to whom it is communicated have a history, pattern, or practice of sharing confidences, such that the recipient of the information knows or reasonably should know that the person communicating the material nonpublic information expects that the recipient will maintain its confidentiality; or (3) Whenever a person receives or obtains material nonpublic information from his or her spouse, parent, child, or sibling; provided , however, that the person receiving or obtaining the information may demonstrate that no duty of trust or confidence existed with respect to the information, by establishing that he or she neither knew nor reasonably should have known that the person who was the source of the information expected that the person would keep the information confidential, because of the parties' history, pattern, or practice of sharing and maintaining confidences, and because there was no agreement or understanding to maintain the confidentiality of the information. 4. Part 243 is added to read as follows: PART 243 -- REGULATION FD Sec. 243.100 General rule regarding selective disclosure. 243.101 Definitions. 243.102 No effect on antifraud liability. 243.103 No effect on Exchange Act reporting status. Authority : 15 U.S.C. 78c, 78i, 78j, 78m, 78o, 78w, 78mm, and 80a-29, unless otherwise noted. § 243.100 General rule regarding selective disclosure . (a) Whenever an issuer, or any person acting on its behalf, discloses any material nonpublic information regarding that issuer or its securities to any person described in paragraph (b)(1) of this section, the issuer shall make public disclosure of that information as provided in § 243.101(e): (1) Simultaneously, in the case of an intentional disclosure; and (2) Promptly, in the case of a non-intentional disclosure. (b)(1) Except as provided in paragraph (b)(2) of this section, paragraph (a) of this section shall apply to a disclosure made to any person outside the issuer: (i) Who is a broker or dealer, or a person associated with a broker or dealer, as those terms are defined in Section 3(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. 78c(a)); (ii) Who is an investment adviser, as that term is defined in Section 202(a)(11) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80b-2(a)(11)); an institutional investment manager, as that term is defined in Section 13(f)(5) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. 78m(f)(5)), that filed a report on Form 13F (17 CFR 249.325) with the Commission for the most recent quarter ended prior to the date of the disclosure; or a person associated with either of the foregoing. For purposes of this paragraph, a "person associated with an investment adviser or institutional investment manager" has the meaning set forth in Section 202(a)(17) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80b-2(a)(17)), assuming for these purposes that an institutional investment manager is an investment adviser; (iii) Who is an investment company, as defined in Section 3 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80a-3), or w
perturbation.justification: The original text used 'shall,' creating a mandatory obligation for parties to advise each other and use commercially reasonable efforts for a mutually agreeable release. The changed text replaces 'shall' with discretionary language like 'may attempt' and 'could then try,' weakening the obligation. This introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the clause, as the parties now have considerable discretion whether to collaborate on press releases. Regulation FD, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, aims to prevent selective disclosure of material nonpublic information. While the snippet doesn't explicitly mandate specific press release procedures, the weakened collaboration requirement in the modified text creates a loophole where a party might release information without proper coordination, potentially leading to selective disclosure.
